text: The surgeon initiates entry through the applied medical gel port positioned at the umbilicus, preparing for the single port splenectomy procedure.
The surgeon observes ridges on the diaphragm, typical in older patients, and draws the spleen inferiorly to reveal a flat area of the diaphragm suitable for sealing.

The Livac device, a 56-millimeter model, is inserted through the applied medical gel port in the umbilicus, providing additional access. The tubing is pulled through the gel port and connected to the Livac connector, with the suction hose clamped.
The surgeon clamps the suction tubing and positions the suction ring within the surgical field.

The surgeon activates suction against the spleen, causing it to lift from the flat area of the diaphragm. As dissection progresses, the splenic hilar vessels become visible. The video is sped up to show the rapid progression of these actions.
The surgeon uses a Ligasure device around the periphery of the spleen to clear attachments, enhancing the definition of the splenic vessels as they approach the hilum.
The surgeon uses a surgical stapler to transect all the splenic vessels en masse, securing them in preparation for removal.
The tail of the pancreas becomes visible as the surgeon continues the dissection near the splenic hilum.
The surgeon observes that the tissue extends up into the splenic hilum, prompting the use of a fine stapler to secure the vessels en masse.
The surgeon moves towards the superior pole of the spleen, using surgical instruments to clear attachments and further expose the area.
The surgeon focuses on clearing the attachments around the splenic hilum using a ligasure device, preparing the area for later stapling of the vessels.
The surgeon guides the Medtronic 60mm tan tri-stapler flush against the vessels entering the spleen, holding it in position for sixty seconds before releasing the firing mechanism.
The surgeon identifies a small residual attachment near the spleen and uses a ligature device to carefully remove it.
The surgeon clamps the suction hose and breaks the seal, likely to manage fluid or air pressure within the surgical field.
The surgeon removes the Livac retractor and introduces a fifteen millimeter retrieval bag through the gel port, using it to scoop up the morselated spleen.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the surgical site after removing the spleen piecemeal. Ropivacaine 0.2% is sprayed over the area, and the diaphragm is observed, showing that a small mark has nearly vanished.
